questions.prompt: An atom of phosphorus-31 contains how many neutrons in its nucleus?
questions.explanation: Neutrons = mass number − atomic number. For P-31: 31 − 15 = 16. The atomic number 15 (also the proton count) is read straight off the Periodic Table on Reference Table S.

questions.prompt: Which electron configuration represents a sulfur atom in an excited state?
questions.explanation: Sulfur's ground-state configuration is 2-8-6. Any arrangement with the same 16 total electrons but a different distribution — like 2-7-7 — represents an excited state where an electron has absorbed energy and jumped to a higher orbital.

questions.prompt: Which model of the atom was proposed first based on the gold-foil scattering experiment?
choices: A solid indivisible sphere with no internal structure
A small dense positively charged nucleus surrounded by mostly empty space
Electrons embedded in a positively charged 'plum pudding'
Electrons orbiting in defined energy levels with quantized energies
questions.correctText: A small dense positively charged nucleus surrounded by mostly empty space
questions.explanation: Rutherford's gold-foil experiment showed most alpha particles passed through undeflected, while a tiny fraction bounced back — evidence that atoms have a small, dense, positively charged nucleus and are mostly empty space. This overturned Thomson's plum pudding model.

questions.prompt: Two atoms have the same atomic number but different mass numbers. These atoms must be:
choices: Different elements in the same period
questions.explanation: Same atomic number means same number of protons, which defines the element. Different mass numbers mean different neutron counts. Atoms of one element that differ only in neutrons are isotopes — for example, carbon-12 and carbon-14.

questions.prompt: As you move from left to right across Period 3 of the Periodic Table, atomic radius generally:
choices: Decreases because nuclear charge increases while electrons are added to the same shell
Increases because each new element gains an extra electron shell
Stays constant because the number of shells does not change
Decreases because elements gain extra neutrons in the nucleus
questions.correctText: Decreases because nuclear charge increases while electrons are added to the same shell
questions.explanation: Across a period, protons are added to the nucleus while electrons fill the same principal shell. The greater nuclear pull contracts the electron cloud. Reference Table S shows Na (190 pm) shrinking to Cl (97 pm) across Period 3.

questions.prompt: Which element on the Periodic Table has the highest electronegativity?
questions.explanation: Electronegativity peaks in the upper-right of the table (excluding noble gases). Reference Table S lists fluorine at 4.0 — the highest of any element — because of its small radius and high effective nuclear charge.

questions.prompt: Which property is characteristic of metalloids such as silicon and germanium?
choices: They exhibit both metallic and nonmetallic properties depending on conditions
They are all gases at standard temperature and pressure
They form negative ions exclusively in chemical reactions
They have completely filled outer electron shells like the noble gases
questions.correctText: They exhibit both metallic and nonmetallic properties depending on conditions
questions.explanation: Metalloids border the staircase line on the Periodic Table and share properties of metals and nonmetals — for example, silicon conducts electricity moderately, making it the basis for semiconductors in modern electronics.

questions.prompt: Why are the noble gases (Group 18) generally unreactive?
choices: Their outer electron shells are completely filled, giving a stable configuration
They have very large atomic radii that prevent bonding
They contain only protons and neutrons in their nuclei
They readily lose all their valence electrons to form positive ions
questions.correctText: Their outer electron shells are completely filled, giving a stable configuration
questions.explanation: Noble gases have full valence shells — helium has 2 outer electrons, the rest have 8. A filled octet is energetically stable, so noble gases have minimal tendency to gain, lose, or share electrons in chemical reactions.

questions.prompt: Which type of bond holds the atoms together in solid sodium chloride (NaCl)?
choices: Ionic bond formed by the transfer of electrons from sodium to chlorine
Nonpolar covalent bond formed by equal sharing of electrons
Metallic bond formed by a sea of delocalized electrons
Hydrogen bond between sodium ions and chloride ions
questions.correctText: Ionic bond formed by the transfer of electrons from sodium to chlorine
questions.explanation: Sodium (metal, low electronegativity 0.9) transfers its single valence electron to chlorine (nonmetal, high electronegativity 3.2). The resulting Na+ and Cl− ions are held in a crystal lattice by electrostatic attraction. The electronegativity difference of 2.3 indicates an ionic bond.

questions.prompt: Which substance has the highest boiling point because of hydrogen bonding between its molecules?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonding occurs when H is bonded directly to F, O, or N. Water's strong O−H···O hydrogen bonds require significant energy to break, giving it a boiling point of 100°C — anomalously high for such a small molecule.

questions.prompt: A molecule of CO2 is nonpolar even though its C=O bonds are polar. The best explanation is:
choices: Its linear shape causes the bond dipoles to cancel symmetrically
Carbon and oxygen have identical electronegativities
The molecule contains no lone pairs of electrons
CO2 forms hydrogen bonds with neighboring molecules
questions.correctText: Its linear shape causes the bond dipoles to cancel symmetrically
questions.explanation: In O=C=O, the two C=O bond dipoles point in exactly opposite directions and have equal magnitude. They cancel, giving a net molecular dipole of zero — so CO2 is nonpolar despite having polar bonds.

questions.prompt: How many moles of water are produced when 2.0 moles of methane burn completely according to CH4 + 2 O2 → CO2 + 2 H2O?
questions.explanation: The mole ratio of CH4 to H2O is 1:2. With 2.0 mol CH4, water produced = 2.0 × 2 = 4.0 mol H2O. Mole ratios from balanced equations are the backbone of stoichiometry.

questions.prompt: Which reaction type is represented by 2 KClO3 → 2 KCl + 3 O2?
questions.explanation: A single compound (KClO3) breaks down into two simpler substances (KCl and O2). This is the signature of a decomposition reaction. Synthesis would be the reverse (A + B → AB).

questions.prompt: The gram formula mass (molar mass) of calcium carbonate (CaCO3) is closest to:
questions.explanation: Using Reference Table S atomic masses: Ca (40.1) + C (12.0) + 3 × O (16.0) = 40 + 12 + 48 = 100 g/mol. CaCO3 (limestone, marble, antacid) is a foundational lab compound.

questions.prompt: What is the molarity of a solution that contains 0.50 mole of NaOH dissolved in enough water to make 250 mL of solution?
questions.explanation: Molarity (M) = moles of solute ÷ liters of solution (Reference Table T). 0.50 mol ÷ 0.250 L = 2.0 M. Always convert mL to L before substituting.

questions.prompt: According to Reference Table G (solubility curves), at 60°C a solution that contains exactly 90 grams of KNO3 per 100 g of water is best described as:
choices: Saturated (the curve passes through 90 g at 60°C)
questions.correctText: Saturated (the curve passes through 90 g at 60°C)
questions.explanation: On Reference Table G, KNO3 reaches a solubility of about 90 g/100 g H2O near 60°C. A solution containing exactly that amount sits right on the curve — saturated. Above the curve = supersaturated; below = unsaturated.

questions.prompt: Which substance, when dissolved in water, would conduct an electric current most strongly?
questions.explanation: Electrolytes are ionic compounds and strong acids/bases that dissociate into mobile ions in water. NaCl dissociates fully into Na+ and Cl−, carrying charge. Glucose, ethanol, and ether are molecular nonelectrolytes — they dissolve without producing ions.

questions.prompt: Adding a catalyst to a chemical reaction system affects the reaction by:
choices: Lowering the activation energy and increasing the reaction rate
Raising the activation energy for both forward and reverse reactions
Shifting the equilibrium toward more products
Increasing the heat of reaction (enthalpy change)
questions.correctText: Lowering the activation energy and increasing the reaction rate
questions.explanation: A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy. Both forward and reverse rates speed up equally, so equilibrium position does not change — only the time required to reach it.

questions.prompt: Given the equilibrium N2(g) + 3 H2(g) ⇌ 2 NH3(g) + heat, what effect will increasing the temperature have on the system?
choices: Shift equilibrium left, decreasing the amount of NH3
Shift equilibrium right, increasing the amount of NH3
Have no effect on the position of equilibrium
Decrease both forward and reverse reaction rates
questions.correctText: Shift equilibrium left, decreasing the amount of NH3
questions.explanation: The forward reaction is exothermic (releases heat). Adding heat (raising temperature) is a stress that Le Chatelier's principle resolves by shifting the system in the direction that consumes heat — the reverse, endothermic direction. NH3 decreases.

questions.prompt: Which change will increase the rate of a reaction between solid zinc and aqueous hydrochloric acid?
choices: Grinding the zinc into a fine powder to increase surface area
Cooling the acid to slow down particle collisions
Diluting the acid to reduce its concentration
Using a smaller volume of HCl while keeping concentration the same
questions.correctText: Grinding the zinc into a fine powder to increase surface area
questions.explanation: Reaction rate factors include concentration, temperature, surface area, and catalysts. Increasing surface area gives acid particles more zinc atoms to collide with per unit time, speeding the reaction. Cooling and diluting both slow the rate.

questions.prompt: According to Arrhenius theory, an acid is a substance that, when dissolved in water:
choices: Releases H+ ions (or H3O+) as the only positive ion
Releases OH− ions as the only negative ion
Donates a pair of electrons to form a covalent bond
Increases the concentration of dissolved nonelectrolytes
questions.correctText: Releases H+ ions (or H3O+) as the only positive ion
questions.explanation: Arrhenius defined acids as substances that ionize in water to produce H+ (hydrogen ion) as the only positive ion. The OH−-producing definition applies to Arrhenius bases. Reference Table K lists common Arrhenius acids.

questions.prompt: A solution with a pH of 3 has a hydrogen-ion concentration that is how many times greater than a solution with a pH of 6?
questions.explanation: The pH scale is logarithmic: each whole pH unit difference equals a 10× change in [H+]. From pH 6 to pH 3 is 3 units lower, so [H+] is 10^3 = 1,000 times greater. Reference Table M shows indicator color ranges across this scale.

questions.prompt: When HCl(aq) reacts with NaOH(aq), the products of this neutralization reaction are:
choices: An acid (HCl) and a base (NaOH) in equilibrium only
A precipitate of NaCl(s) and a gas of HOH
questions.explanation: Acid + base → salt + water is the defining pattern of neutralization. HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H2O. The H+ from the acid combines with OH− from the base to form water, while the spectator ions form the salt.

questions.prompt: In the reaction Zn(s) + Cu2+(aq) → Zn2+(aq) + Cu(s), the zinc atom is:
choices: Oxidized — it loses two electrons and its oxidation number increases from 0 to +2
Reduced — it gains two electrons and its oxidation number decreases from 0 to −2
Acting as a spectator ion with no change in oxidation number
Acting as a catalyst that speeds the reaction without changing
questions.correctText: Oxidized — it loses two electrons and its oxidation number increases from 0 to +2
questions.explanation: Zinc starts as Zn(0) and ends as Zn2+(+2), so it loses 2 electrons. LEO = Lose Electrons Oxidation. The copper ion (Cu2+ → Cu0) gains those electrons and is reduced. Reference Table J's activity series shows Zn above Cu, confirming Zn can displace Cu.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes the key difference between a voltaic (galvanic) cell and an electrolytic cell?
choices: A voltaic cell generates electricity from a spontaneous redox reaction; an electrolytic cell uses electricity to force a nonspontaneous reaction
Both cells produce electricity from spontaneous reactions
Electrolytic cells use only metallic conductors; voltaic cells use only nonmetallic conductors
Voltaic cells are used for electroplating; electrolytic cells are used in batteries
questions.correctText: A voltaic cell generates electricity from a spontaneous redox reaction; an electrolytic cell uses electricity to force a nonspontaneous reaction
questions.explanation: Voltaic cells (batteries) convert chemical energy from spontaneous redox into electrical energy. Electrolytic cells do the opposite — they consume electrical energy from an external source to drive a nonspontaneous reaction, like electroplating or refining aluminum.

questions.prompt: According to Reference Table J (activity series), which metal can spontaneously displace silver from a solution of silver nitrate?
choices: Copper (located above silver in the activity series)
Gold (located below silver in the activity series)
Platinum (located below silver in the activity series)
None of the listed metals can displace silver
questions.correctText: Copper (located above silver in the activity series)
questions.explanation: On Reference Table J, a metal will displace any metal listed below it. Copper sits above silver, so Cu(s) + 2 AgNO3(aq) → Cu(NO3)2(aq) + 2 Ag(s) proceeds spontaneously. Gold and platinum, both below Ag, cannot displace it.

questions.prompt: Which compound is an alkene with the molecular formula C3H6?
choices: Propene (CH2=CH−CH3) — contains a carbon-carbon double bond
questions.correctText: Propene (CH2=CH−CH3) — contains a carbon-carbon double bond
questions.explanation: Alkenes fit the general formula CnH2n and contain at least one C=C double bond. For n=3: C3H6 = propene. Propane is C3H8 (alkane, CnH2n+2), and propyne is C3H4 (alkyne, CnH2n−2). Reference Table Q lists these series.

questions.prompt: Which functional group is present in the compound CH3COOH (acetic acid)?
questions.explanation: The −COOH group is the carboxyl functional group, defining carboxylic acids. Reference Table R shows the structural formula. Acetic acid is the active component in vinegar and gives it its sharp taste.

questions.prompt: The reaction between an alcohol and an organic acid that produces an ester and water is called:
questions.explanation: Esterification: alcohol + carboxylic acid → ester + water. For example, ethanol + acetic acid → ethyl acetate + H2O. Reference Table P lists this among organic reaction types. Saponification is the reverse — a base hydrolyzing an ester.

questions.prompt: Which type of nuclear decay emits a particle identical to a helium-4 nucleus?
questions.explanation: An alpha particle is a helium-4 nucleus (2 protons + 2 neutrons), symbol ⁴₂He or α. Reference Table O lists decay-particle symbols. Beta-minus is an electron, positron is a positive electron, and gamma is high-energy electromagnetic radiation.

questions.prompt: An isotope has a half-life of 8 days. What fraction of an original sample remains after 24 days?
questions.explanation: Number of half-lives = total time ÷ half-life = 24 ÷ 8 = 3. Fraction remaining = (1/2)^3 = 1/8. Reference Table N lists half-lives for common isotopes; iodine-131 (8.02 days) is the canonical example.

questions.prompt: In the nuclear equation ²³⁵₉₂U + ¹₀n → ¹⁴¹₅₆Ba + ⁹²₃₆Kr + 3 ¹₀n, this process is best classified as:
choices: Nuclear fission — a heavy nucleus splits into smaller nuclei
Nuclear fusion — small nuclei combine into a larger one
Alpha decay — emission of a helium-4 nucleus
Beta decay — conversion of a neutron into a proton plus electron
questions.correctText: Nuclear fission — a heavy nucleus splits into smaller nuclei
questions.explanation: Uranium-235 absorbs a neutron and splits into barium-141 and krypton-92 plus three more neutrons. This is fission — the basis of nuclear reactors. Fusion is the opposite (light nuclei combining, as in the Sun).

questions.prompt: A student measures the boiling point of a liquid to be 102.0°C; the accepted value is 100.0°C. The percent error of this measurement is:
questions.explanation: Percent error = |measured − accepted| / accepted × 100 (Reference Table T). |102.0 − 100.0| / 100.0 × 100 = 2.0 / 100.0 × 100 = 2.0%. Always use the accepted value as the denominator.

questions.prompt: How much heat is required to raise the temperature of 50.0 g of water by 20.0°C? (Specific heat of water = 4.18 J/g·°C)
questions.explanation: q = mCΔT (Reference Table T). q = 50.0 g × 4.18 J/g·°C × 20.0°C = 4,180 J. This formula handles heat transfer when no phase change occurs; mHf and mHv handle melting and vaporization respectively.

questions.prompt: Which measurement contains four significant figures?
questions.explanation: Leading zeros are not significant; trailing zeros after a decimal point ARE significant. 0.04050: the '4', '0' (between digits), '5', and trailing '0' all count → 4 sig figs. 405 has 3; 0.045 has 2; 4500 (no decimal) is ambiguous, typically 2.

questions.prompt: A gas at 2.0 atm and 300 K occupies 4.0 L. According to the combined gas law, what volume will it occupy at 1.0 atm and 600 K?
questions.explanation: Combined gas law (Reference Table T): P1V1/T1 = P2V2/T2. Solve for V2: V2 = (P1V1T2) / (P2T1) = (2.0 × 4.0 × 600) / (1.0 × 300) = 4800 / 300 = 16 L.

questions.prompt: On a heating curve for water, the temperature stays constant from −2°C while ice melts to liquid water. This 'plateau' is best explained by the fact that:
choices: Heat energy is being used to break intermolecular forces, not raise kinetic energy
The thermometer has been calibrated incorrectly during the experiment
Heat is being absorbed only by the container, not by the water
All molecular motion has stopped during the phase change
questions.correctText: Heat energy is being used to break intermolecular forces, not raise kinetic energy
questions.explanation: During a phase change at constant pressure, added heat goes into overcoming intermolecular attractions (potential energy), not into kinetic energy. Temperature reflects average kinetic energy, so it stays flat until the phase change is complete. The heat absorbed equals m × Hf (or m × Hv).

questions.prompt: Which set of conditions defines standard temperature and pressure (STP) according to the Reference Tables?
questions.explanation: Reference Table A defines STP as 273.15 K (0°C) and 101.3 kPa (1 atmosphere). At STP, one mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.4 L. The molar-volume constant only applies under STP conditions.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a postulate of the kinetic molecular theory of an ideal gas?
choices: Gas particles undergo perfectly elastic collisions and have negligible volume
Gas particles attract one another strongly at room temperature
All gas particles in a sample move with exactly the same speed
Gas particles take up most of the available volume in a container
questions.correctText: Gas particles undergo perfectly elastic collisions and have negligible volume
questions.explanation: KMT postulates: (1) gas particles are in constant random motion; (2) they have negligible volume compared to the container; (3) collisions are perfectly elastic (no net energy lost); (4) intermolecular forces are negligible. Real gases deviate from these assumptions at high pressure and low temperature.
